title: BioUtils: A Case Study in Transcriptomic Analysis of Renal Cell Carcinoma
text: Understanding genes expressed differently diseased versus healthy tissue one central questions modern molecular biology. cell becomes cancerous, hundreds thousands genes may change activity levels, becoming dramatically active, others effectively silenced. Identifying changes essential discovering drives disease, finding diagnostic biomarkers, ultimately developing targeted therapies. vignette walks complete transcriptomic analysis renal cell carcinoma (RCC), common form kidney cancer, using publicly available gene expression microarray data NCBI Gene Expression Omnibus (GEO). dataset, GDS507, contains expression profiles 17 samples, mix clear cell RCC tumor tissue matched normal kidney tissue, measured across ~22,000 probe sets Affymetrix HG-U133A microarray. use BioUtils package carry full analytical workflow: data import, quality control, differential expression analysis, visualization, co-expression analysis, multi-gene predictive modeling. step explained terms code means biologically.

previous_headings: Part 1: Data Import and Quality Control
title: BioUtils: A Case Study in Transcriptomic Analysis of Renal Cell Carcinoma
text: load data, helps understand working . DNA microarray glass slide chip printed tens thousands short DNA sequences called probes, designed bind RNA transcribed specific gene. biological sample processed washed chip, messenger RNA active genes hybridizes matching probe emits fluorescent signal. brightness spot reflects actively gene transcribed, expression level. result table ~22,000 numbers per sample, one probe chip, representing transcriptional activity cell moment sample collected. job extract biologically meaningful signal high-dimensional data.

previous_headings: Part 1: Data Import and Quality Control
title: BioUtils: A Case Study in Transcriptomic Analysis of Renal Cell Carcinoma
text: GEO archive distributes datasets SOFT format files. BioUtils reads directly using load.geo.soft(), wraps GEOquery parse file return structured ExpressionSet object: log.transform = TRUE argument important . Affymetrix arrays report raw intensity values span several orders magnitude right-skewed. Applying log2 transformation compresses range, makes data symmetric, means differences expression become differences fold change, 1-unit increase log2 scale represents doubling expression. standard preprocessing Affymetrix data necessary statistical testing.

previous_headings: Part 1: Data Import and Quality Control
title: BioUtils: A Case Study in Transcriptomic Analysis of Renal Cell Carcinoma
text: extract.expression() decomposes ExpressionSet three components used throughout BioUtils: returns named list : geo$expression - 22,645 17 matrix log2-transformed probe intensities. row probe (specific DNA sequence chip), column patient sample. geo$phenotype - data frame sample metadata. importantly us, disease.state column identifies sample either "RCC" "normal". geo$gene - data frame probe annotations mapping probe ID gene targets (gene symbol, full title, chromosomal location, etc.).

previous_headings: Part 1: Data Import and Quality Control
what: 1.4 Principal Component Analysis for Quality Control
title: BioUtils: A Case Study in Transcriptomic Analysis of Renal Cell Carcinoma
text: 22,000 variables 17 samples, data far high-dimensional inspect directly. Principal Component Analysis (PCA) standard first step understanding global structure dataset. finds axes maximum variance data projects sample onto axes, similar samples cluster together 2D plot. analysis, want confirm largest source variation data biological signal care , disease state, rather technical artifacts like batch effects sample processing date.  pca.plot() runs PCA transposed expression matrix (samples observations probes variables), extracts first two principal components, plots sample point colored disease state. well-separated PCA plot, RCC normal samples forming distinct clusters, tells us transcriptional differences tumor normal tissue large enough dominate global variance structure dataset. reassuring quality control check: means biology strong statistical tests good power detect . instead samples mixed randomly clustered something unrelated (technician ID, plate number), need investigate correct proceeding.

title: BioUtils: A Case Study in Transcriptomic Analysis of Renal Cell Carcinoma
text: want know, 22,645 probes, whether average expression level differs significantly RCC normal tissue. naive approach, run t-test probe separately, fundamental problem: run 22,645 independent tests 5% significance threshold, expect get roughly 1,132 false positives chance alone, even nothing truly different. multiple testing problem.

title: BioUtils: A Case Study in Transcriptomic Analysis of Renal Cell Carcinoma
text: BioUtils uses limma (Linear Models Microarray data) framework differential expression analysis, solves problems elegantly: Rather testing gene independently, limma fits linear model probes simultaneously. key innovation empirical Bayes moderation: borrows variance information across genes produce stabilized estimate within-group variability probe. Genes samples noisy measurements benefit pooled information, prevents genes accidentally small variance estimates generating artificially inflated t-statistics. result, called TopTable, data frame one row per probe, sorted evidence differential expression. important columns : logFC - log2 fold change RCC normal tissue. value 2 means RCC samples express gene 4-fold higher average. value -1 means expression halved RCC. adj.P.Val - p-value Benjamini-Hochberg correction multiple testing. controls false discovery rate (FDR): adj.P.Val 0.05 means expect 5% probes call significant false positives, rather 5% tests wrong.

title: BioUtils: A Case Study in Transcriptomic Analysis of Renal Cell Carcinoma
text: volcano plot standard visualization differential expression result set. places every probe two-dimensional space: fold change x-axis (large difference ) statistical significance y-axis (confident difference). biologically interesting genes, large effect, high confidence, appear upper corners.  dashed lines mark thresholds: genes must absolute log2 fold change greater 1 (2-fold change) FDR-adjusted p-value 0.05 colored differentially expressed. Genes red upregulated RCC relative normal; genes blue downregulated. kidney cancer, might expect see upregulation genes involved angiogenesis (tumor blood vessel formation), glycolysis (many kidney cancers switch metabolism even normal oxygen levels, Warburg effect), cell proliferation, genes involved normal kidney function differentiation may downregulated.

title: BioUtils: A Case Study in Transcriptomic Analysis of Renal Cell Carcinoma
text: now extract probe IDs significantly differentially expressed genes carry forward deeper analysis: get.gene.name() performs reverse lookup find.probe..gene(), given probe IDs TopTable, retrieves human-readable gene title annotation data frame. filtering step removes probes present array gene annotation database (common older array designs).
code: # Extract the 10 most significant probe IDs (row names of the TopTable) top.probes <- rownames(head(de.results[order(de.results$adj.P.Val), ], 10))  # Resolve probe IDs to human-readable gene names top.genes <- get.gene.name(geo$gene, top.probes)  # Some probes on the array do not map to annotated genes, remove those annotated.mask <- which(top.genes != "") top.probes <- top.probes[annotated.mask] top.genes <- top.genes[annotated.mask]  cat("Top differentially expressed genes:\n") #> Top differentially expressed genes: print(top.genes) #>  [1] "6-phosphofructo-2-kinase/fructose-2,6-biphosphatase 2" #>  [2] "mucin 15, cell surface associated"                     #>  [3] "transcription factor CP2-like 1"                       #>  [4] "potassium voltage-gated channel subfamily J member 10" #>  [5] "transcription factor CP2-like 1"                       #>  [6] "transcription factor 21"                               #>  [7] "cortexin 3"                                            #>  [8] "solute carrier family 4 member 9"                      #>  [9] "aquaporin 2"                                           #> [10] "transmembrane protein 213"

title: BioUtils: A Case Study in Transcriptomic Analysis of Renal Cell Carcinoma
text: limma TopTable tells us genes differentially expressed. Now use BioUtils understand expressed, magnitude, direction, robustness gene’s difference.

title: BioUtils: A Case Study in Transcriptomic Analysis of Renal Cell Carcinoma
text: get.gene.expression() slices rows full expression matrix corresponding selected probes. build.analysis.df() pivots wide matrix (probes samples) long-format data frame row represents one gene-sample observation, columns gene name, expression value, disease state group. format required downstream analysis visualization functions.
code: # Retrieve expression values for all top probes as a matrix expr.mat <- get.gene.expression(geo$expression, top.probes)  # Build the long-format analysis data frame df.multi <- build.analysis.df(expr.mat, geo$phenotype, geo$gene)

title: BioUtils: A Case Study in Transcriptomic Analysis of Renal Cell Carcinoma
text: multi-gene mode, gene.analysis.plot() creates faceted panel, one violin/boxplot per gene, allowing visual comparison expression patterns across entire candidate set . useful identifying genes show large, clean separation groups show overlap outlier-driven differences.
code: # Faceted violin + boxplot across all top genes simultaneously gene.analysis.plot(df.multi)

title: BioUtils: A Case Study in Transcriptomic Analysis of Renal Cell Carcinoma
text: gene showing dramatic separation, perform full statistical analysis:  single-gene mode, gene.analysis.plot() runs analyze.gene() internally annotates plot key statistical results.
code: # Subset to a single gene of interest gene.of.interest <- get.gene.name(geo$gene, top.probes[1], use.symbols=TRUE) df.single <- df.multi[which(df.multi$gene == gene.of.interest), ]  # Annotated single-gene visualization gene.analysis.plot(df.single)

title: BioUtils: A Case Study in Transcriptomic Analysis of Renal Cell Carcinoma
text: analyze.gene() integrates multiple statistical perspectives single object. worth understanding component contributes: adaptive t-test (result$raw$parametric): BioUtils first runs var.test() check whether two groups equal variances. (common occurrence gene expression data, disease states often increase expression variability), automatically uses Welch’s t-test, assume equal variances, rather standard Student’s t-test. prevents common source inflated false positives. Cohen’s d (result$effect.size): p-value alone tell whether statistically significant difference biologically meaningful. 17 samples, even tiny difference means can reach statistical significance. Cohen’s d standardized effect size, difference group means divided pooled standard deviation, puts magnitude difference context. d 0.2 small; 0.5 moderate; 0.8 large. clinical context, genes small effect sizes unlikely serve robust biomarkers even highly significant. Bootstrapped confidence interval (result$confidence.interval): confidence interval around Cohen’s d quantifies uncertainty true effect size given sample size. narrow CI (e.g., [0.8, 1.2]) means precise estimate; wide CI (e.g., [0.1, 1.5]) means true effect anywhere negligible large, cautious strong claims. Wilcoxon rank-sum test (result$nonparametric.p): t-test assumes expression values roughly normally distributed within group. Gene expression data often violates assumption, particularly one group outlier samples. Wilcoxon test makes distributional assumptions provides independent check. tests agree (result$robustness), can confident result. disagree, t-test significant, Wilcoxon , result may driven distributional differences rather genuine shift population mean.
code:
result <- analyze.gene(df.single) cat(result$interpretation) #> The difference is statistically significant with a large effect size. #> Result is robust across parametric and nonparametric tests. #> Potentially biologically meaningful. # Inspect individual components cat("Effect size (Cohen's d):", round(result$effect.size, 3), "
") #> Effect size (Cohen's d): 2.312 cat("Effect size class:      ", result$effect.size.class, "
") #> Effect size class:       large cat("95% CI:                 [",     round(result$confidence.interval$lower, 3), ",",     round(result$confidence.interval$upper, 3), "]
") #> 95% CI:                 [ 1.53 , 4.54 ] cat("Parametric p-value:     ", signif(result$p.value, 3), "
") #> Parametric p-value:      0.000254 cat("Nonparametric p-value:  ", signif(result$nonparametric.p, 3), "
") #> Nonparametric p-value:   0.000329 cat("Robustness:             ", result$robustness, "
") #> Robustness:              robust across parametric and nonparametric tests cat("Biological assessment:  ", result$biological.relevance, "
") #> Biological assessment:   Potentially biologically meaningful

title: BioUtils: A Case Study in Transcriptomic Analysis of Renal Cell Carcinoma
text: far every analysis focused individual genes, ones differentially expressed, large effects , ones jointly predict disease state. genes act isolation. participate coordinated biological pathways: cascades molecular events together accomplish cellular function. limma TopTable tells us genes change; Gene Set Enrichment Analysis (GSEA) tells us changes mean level biology.

title: BioUtils: A Case Study in Transcriptomic Analysis of Renal Cell Carcinoma
text: gene set simply curated list genes known participate biological process. Molecular Signatures Database (MSigDB) maintains thousands sets organized collections. widely used collection disease studies Hallmark collection (category = "H"), contains 50 gene sets representing well-defined biological states, things like “HYPOXIA”, “MYC_TARGETS_V1”, “INFLAMMATORY_RESPONSE”, “EPITHELIAL_MESENCHYMAL_TRANSITION”. set assembled expert curation coherent minimally overlapping others. kidney cancer specifically, might expect see strong enrichment hypoxia-related gene sets. Clear cell RCC commonly inactivates VHL tumor suppressor gene, normally targets HIF1transcription factor degradation. Without VHL, HIF1accumulates drives transcriptional program mimicking chronic oxygen deprivation, activating genes blood vessel formation, glucose uptake, anaerobic metabolism. one best-characterized molecular signatures oncology, GSEA ideal tool detecting .

title: BioUtils: A Case Study in Transcriptomic Analysis of Renal Cell Carcinoma
text: use msigdbr package download Hallmark gene sets named list, format run.gsea() expects:
code: # Download Hallmark gene sets for Homo sapiens hallmark.df <- msigdbr::msigdbr(species = "Homo sapiens", category = "H")  # Convert to a named list: pathway name -> character vector of gene symbols pathways <- split(hallmark.df$gene_symbol, hallmark.df$gs_name)  # Each element is a vector of gene symbols belonging to that pathway cat("Number of Hallmark gene sets:", length(pathways), "\n") #> Number of Hallmark gene sets: 50 cat("Example - first 6 genes in HALLMARK_HYPOXIA:\n") #> Example - first 6 genes in HALLMARK_HYPOXIA: print(head(pathways[["HALLMARK_HYPOXIA"]])) #> [1] "ACKR3"   "ADM"     "ADORA2B" "AK4"     "AKAP12"  "ALDOA"

title: BioUtils: A Case Study in Transcriptomic Analysis of Renal Cell Carcinoma
text: GSEA works ranked gene list, genes experiment ordered upregulated downregulated, rather filtered list significant hits. important distinction simpler enrichment methods ask “pathway genes -represented among significant hits?” GSEA asks “pathway genes cluster top bottom full ranked list?” makes sensitive coordinated shifts across entire pathway even individual gene clears significance threshold. ranking metric used run.gsea() log2 fold change limma TopTable: genes top upregulated RCC relative normal, genes bottom downregulated.
code: gsea.results <- run.gsea(de.results, geo$gene, pathways, min.size = 15, max.size = 500)  # Sort by adjusted p-value and inspect the top enriched pathways gsea.results <- gsea.results[order(gsea.results$padj), ] print(head(gsea.results[, c("pathway", "NES", "pval", "padj", "size")], 10)) #>                                        pathway       NES         pval #>                                         <char>     <num>        <num> #>  1:         HALLMARK_INTERFERON_GAMMA_RESPONSE  2.583616 5.087170e-11 #>  2:               HALLMARK_ALLOGRAFT_REJECTION  2.347025 7.457370e-07 #>  3:           HALLMARK_TNFA_SIGNALING_VIA_NFKB  2.169457 5.803936e-06 #>  4:         HALLMARK_INTERFERON_ALPHA_RESPONSE  2.037789 1.193410e-04 #>  5: HALLMARK_EPITHELIAL_MESENCHYMAL_TRANSITION  1.841121 6.403048e-04 #>  6:                           HALLMARK_HYPOXIA  1.793828 1.374159e-03 #>  7:           HALLMARK_IL6_JAK_STAT3_SIGNALING  1.837732 1.446032e-03 #>  8:         HALLMARK_OXIDATIVE_PHOSPHORYLATION -1.771289 1.327165e-03 #>  9:             HALLMARK_INFLAMMATORY_RESPONSE  1.785555 1.768716e-03 #> 10:             HALLMARK_XENOBIOTIC_METABOLISM -1.788475 2.267151e-03 #>             padj  size #>            <num> <int> #>  1: 2.289227e-09    67 #>  2: 1.677908e-05    38 #>  3: 8.705904e-05    59 #>  4: 1.342586e-03    32 #>  5: 5.762743e-03    64 #>  6: 8.133928e-03    63 #>  7: 8.133928e-03    28 #>  8: 8.133928e-03    56 #>  9: 8.843581e-03    58 #> 10: 1.020218e-02    50

title: BioUtils: A Case Study in Transcriptomic Analysis of Renal Cell Carcinoma
text: two important columns GSEA output : NES (Normalized Enrichment Score): core statistic GSEA. positive NES means gene set enriched among genes upregulated RCC, genes belonging pathway tend cluster top ranked list. negative NES means pathway enriched among downregulated genes, suppressed RCC relative normal tissue. normalization accounts gene set size, NES values comparable across sets. padj: NES tested significance permuting gene labels recomputing enrichment scores null hypothesis genes randomly distributed ranked list. padj resulting p-value Benjamini-Hochberg correction across tested pathways. leadingEdge: subset genes pathway contribute driving enrichment score. biologically interesting genes within enriched pathway, ones “leading edge” ranked list. strong candidates follow-analyze.gene(). closes analytical loop: GSEA identifies biological programs dysregulated RCC pathway level, leading edge genes point us back individual-gene tools, analyze.gene() gene.analysis.plot(), characterize important drivers within pathway.
code: # Extract the leading edge genes of the top enriched pathway top.pathway <- gsea.results$pathway[1] leading.genes <- unlist(gsea.results$leadingEdge[1])  cat("Top enriched pathway:", top.pathway, "\n") #> Top enriched pathway: HALLMARK_INTERFERON_GAMMA_RESPONSE cat("Leading edge genes:\n") #> Leading edge genes: print(leading.genes) #>  [1] "ST8SIA4" "IRF1"    "SAMHD1"  "SSPN"    "GBP4"    "SLAMF7"  "XAF1"    #>  [8] "IL18BP"  "NLRC5"   "RSAD2"   "LATS2"   "LCP2"    "CMPK2"   "OAS3"    #> [15] "PTPN2"   "ZNFX1"   "RNF213"  "SAMD9L"  "CMKLR1"  "HELZ2"   "RAPGEF6" #> [22] "NAMPT"   "IFIT3"   "STAT1"   "IFNAR2"  "PARP14"  "SP110"   "PELI1"   #> [29] "CD274"   "EPSTI1"  "OAS2"    "IFIT2"   "RBCK1"   "ZBP1"    "EIF2AK2" #> [36] "STAT2"   "BANK1"   "PML"     "TRIM25"  "NOD1"    "LYSMD2"  "ARID5B"  # Find probes for the leading edge genes and perform single-gene deep-dives leading.probes <- find.probe.by.gene(geo$gene, leading.genes) leading.probes <- leading.probes[leading.probes != ""]  if(length(leading.probes) > 0) {   leading.expr <- get.gene.expression(geo$expression, leading.probes)   df.leading <- build.analysis.df(leading.expr, geo$phenotype, geo$gene)   gene.analysis.plot(df.leading) }

title: BioUtils: A Case Study in Transcriptomic Analysis of Renal Cell Carcinoma
text: Individual gene analysis tells us gene isolation. biology system, genes act alone. connected regulatory networks, share transcription factors, participate common pathways. BioUtils provides two complementary tools exploring multi-gene relationships.

title: BioUtils: A Case Study in Transcriptomic Analysis of Renal Cell Carcinoma
text: Genes regulated together tend show correlated expression patterns across samples: one goes , goes . co-expression relationships can suggest shared regulatory control, functional partnership, membership biological pathway.  gene.correlation.matrix() computes full pairwise correlation matrix across samples. correlation.heatmap.plot() visualizes clustered heatmap, hierarchical clustering reorders rows columns genes similar co-expression profiles placed next . Red cells indicate strongly co-expressed gene pairs; blue cells indicate anti-correlated pairs (one goes , tends go ). Clusters co-expressed genes visible heatmap often correspond genes biological pathway control transcription factor. renal cell carcinoma context, might see cluster angiogenesis-related genes (driven HIF1pathway activation, common RCC) moving together, cluster genes involved fatty acid metabolism.
code: # Compute pairwise Pearson correlations between our top probes cor.mat <- gene.correlation.matrix(geo$expression, top.probes, method = "pearson")  # Visualize with hierarchical clustering correlation.heatmap.plot(cor.mat, gene.names = get.gene.name(geo$gene, top.probes, use.symbols=TRUE))

what: 5.2 LASSO Regression for Predictive Biomarker Discovery
title: BioUtils: A Case Study in Transcriptomic Analysis of Renal Cell Carcinoma
text: Co-expression tells us correlations. separate question : genes, taken together, best discriminate RCC normal tissue? classification problem, fit.lasso() comes . LASSO? Standard logistic regression applied : probes samples, many top genes correlated (shown heatmap). LASSO (Least Absolute Shrinkage Selection Operator) adds penalty term logistic regression forces gene coefficients exactly zero, keeping independent predictive value selected genes accounted . cv.glmnet() determines optimal penalty strength 10-fold cross-validation. lambda.1se, regularized model within one standard error minimum cross-validation error, LASSO returns sparsest model still predicts well, tends interpretable generalizable. genes non-zero coefficients selected output BioUtils’s answer question: “measure handful genes diagnose RCC biopsy, ones give information?” strong candidates validation diagnostic biomarkers. complementary limma DE analysis: limma identifies genes differ individually groups, LASSO identifies minimal set genes jointly carry discriminative information. gene might highly significant limma dropped LASSO information redundant another gene panel.
code: # Encode disease state as a binary outcome variable phenotype.binary <- ifelse(geo$phenotype$disease.state == "RCC", 1, 0)  # Fit LASSO logistic regression across all top probes simultaneously lasso.fit <- fit.lasso(geo$expression, phenotype.binary)  # Extract genes selected by LASSO at the 1-standard-error lambda coef.mat <- coef(lasso.fit, s = "lambda.1se") selected  <- coef.mat[coef.mat[, 1] != 0, ] print(selected) #> (Intercept)   222405_at   223798_at   226733_at   226851_at   227238_at  #>  4.58244434 -0.49969262  0.59096443 -0.59527813 -0.02235620 -0.05695169  #>   227367_at   228581_at   229529_at   231391_at  #>  1.09276938 -0.41421102 -0.43068340 -0.03464796

title: BioUtils: A Case Study in Transcriptomic Analysis of Renal Cell Carcinoma
text: full analysis, raw SOFT file multi-gene biomarker panel, can expressed coherent pipeline:
code: library(BioUtils)  # == 1. Import ================================================================= eset <- load.geo.soft("", "GDS507", log.transform = TRUE) geo <- extract.expression(eset)  # == 2. Quality Control ======================================================== pca.plot(geo$expression, geo$phenotype, color.by = "disease.state") # == 3. Genome-Wide Differential Expression ==================================== de.results <- run.limma.de(geo, condition.col = "disease.state") volcano.plot(de.results, fc.threshold = 1, fdr.threshold = 0.05) # == 4. Select Top Candidates ================================================== top.probes <- rownames(head(de.results[order(de.results$adj.P.Val), ], 10)) top.genes <- get.gene.name(geo$gene, top.probes) valid.mask <- which(top.genes != "") top.probes <- top.probes[valid.mask] top.genes <- top.genes[valid.mask]  # == 5. Build Analysis Data Frame ============================================== expr.mat <- get.gene.expression(geo$expression, top.probes) df.multi <- build.analysis.df(expr.mat, geo$phenotype, geo$gene)  # == 6. Multi-Gene Visualization =============================================== gene.analysis.plot(df.multi) # == 7. Single-Gene Deep-Dive ================================================== df.single <- df.multi[which(df.multi$gene == get.gene.name(geo$gene, top.probes[1], use.symbols=TRUE)), ] gene.analysis.plot(df.single) result <- analyze.gene(df.single) cat(result$interpretation) #> The difference is statistically significant with a large effect size. #> Result is robust across parametric and nonparametric tests. #> Potentially biologically meaningful.  # == 8. Co-expression Analysis ================================================= cor.mat <- gene.correlation.matrix(geo$expression, top.probes, method = "pearson") correlation.heatmap.plot(cor.mat, gene.names = get.gene.name(geo$gene, top.probes, use.symbols=TRUE)) # == 9. Pathway Enrichment ===================================================== hallmark.df <- msigdbr::msigdbr(species = "Homo sapiens", category = "H") pathways <- split(hallmark.df$gene_symbol, hallmark.df$gs_name) gsea.results <- run.gsea(de.results, geo$gene, pathways) gsea.results <- gsea.results[order(gsea.results$padj), ] print(head(gsea.results[, c("pathway", "NES", "pval", "padj")], 10)) #>                                        pathway       NES         pval #>                                         <char>     <num>        <num> #>  1:         HALLMARK_INTERFERON_GAMMA_RESPONSE  2.540590 5.472170e-11 #>  2:               HALLMARK_ALLOGRAFT_REJECTION  2.315882 7.376635e-07 #>  3:           HALLMARK_TNFA_SIGNALING_VIA_NFKB  2.129536 6.955686e-06 #>  4:         HALLMARK_INTERFERON_ALPHA_RESPONSE  2.006157 2.358589e-04 #>  5: HALLMARK_EPITHELIAL_MESENCHYMAL_TRANSITION  1.800980 5.363443e-04 #>  6:                           HALLMARK_HYPOXIA  1.745162 1.378414e-03 #>  7:             HALLMARK_INFLAMMATORY_RESPONSE  1.751200 1.408097e-03 #>  8:             HALLMARK_XENOBIOTIC_METABOLISM -1.749817 1.892028e-03 #>  9:           HALLMARK_IL6_JAK_STAT3_SIGNALING  1.811958 2.273682e-03 #> 10:                  HALLMARK_MTORC1_SIGNALING  1.633314 3.106323e-03 #>             padj #>            <num> #>  1: 2.462476e-09 #>  2: 1.659743e-05 #>  3: 1.043353e-04 #>  4: 2.653413e-03 #>  5: 4.827098e-03 #>  6: 9.052053e-03 #>  7: 9.052053e-03 #>  8: 1.064266e-02 #>  9: 1.136841e-02 #> 10: 1.270768e-02  # == 10. Multi-Gene Predictive Modeling ======================================== phenotype.binary <- ifelse(geo$phenotype$disease.state == "RCC", 1, 0) lasso.fit <- fit.lasso(geo$expression, phenotype.binary) coef.mat <- coef(lasso.fit, s = "lambda.1se") selected <- coef.mat[coef.mat[, 1] != 0, ] print(selected) #> (Intercept)   222405_at   223798_at   226733_at   226851_at   227238_at  #>  4.33535108 -0.46760073  0.55402455 -0.58793657 -0.01273469 -0.05691449  #>   227367_at   228581_at   229529_at   231391_at  #>  1.05913445 -0.40589374 -0.40796875 -0.03030960

what: BioUtils: Tools for Biological Data Analysis and Visualization
title: BioUtils: Tools for Biological Data Analysis and Visualization
text: BioUtils end--end R toolkit analyzing gene expression data GEO datasets. provides unified workflow differential expression, statistical testing, visualization, biological interpretation.

title: BioUtils: Tools for Biological Data Analysis and Visualization
text: Load preprocess GEO datasets Differential expression analysis using limma Visualization (PCA, volcano plots, gene-level plots) Statistical testing (adaptive t-test, effect size, bootstrapped CI) Gene set enrichment analysis (GSEA) Machine learning (LASSO biomarker selection) Gene co-expression analysis

title: BioUtils: Tools for Biological Data Analysis and Visualization
BioUtils: Tools for Biological Data Analysis and Visualization
code: # Load data eset <- load.geo.soft("GDS507.soft", log.transform = TRUE) geo <- extract.expression(eset)  # PCA visualization pca.plot(geo$expression, geo$phenotype, color.by = "disease.state")  # Differential expression de.results <- run.limma.de(geo)  # Volcano plot volcano.plot(de.results, fc.threshold = 0.3)  # Select top genes top.genes <- head(rownames(de.results[order(de.results$adj.P.Val), ]), 5) probe.ids <- find.probe.by.gene(geo$gene, top.genes)  # Single gene analysis expr <- get.gene.expression(geo$expression, probe.ids[1]) df <- build.analysis.df(expr, geo$phenotype, geo$gene) gene.analysis.plot(df)  # LASSO model phenotype.binary <- ifelse(geo$phenotype$disease.state == "disease", 1, 0) lasso.fit <- fit.lasso(geo$expression, phenotype.binary)

what: BioUtils: Tools for Biological Data Analysis and Visualization — BioUtils-package
title: BioUtils: Tools for Biological Data Analysis and Visualization — BioUtils-package
text: BioUtils provides collection tools preprocessing biological datasets, performing statistical analyses, generating visualizations. See vignette("BioUtils") full workflow walkthrough.

title: BioUtils: Tools for Biological Data Analysis and Visualization — BioUtils-package
text: Maintainer: Spencer Treadway spencer.treadway08@gmail.com

title: Perform Adaptive T-Test — adaptive.t.test
text: Automatically selects Welch's Student's t-test based result variance equality test, returns unified result structure regardless branch taken.

title: Perform Adaptive T-Test — adaptive.t.test
Perform Adaptive T-Test — adaptive.t.test
text: df data frame containing least two columns: expression Numeric vector gene expression values. group Character factor vector exactly two group labels. alpha Numeric. Significance level variance equality test (var.test()). Default 0.05.

title: Perform Adaptive T-Test — adaptive.t.test
text: named list three elements: variance.test result object var.test() oneway.test(), depending branch taken. t.test result object t.test(). p.value Numeric. p-value t-test.

title: Perform Adaptive T-Test — adaptive.t.test
text: var.test() returns p-value alpha, variances considered unequal Welch's t-test (var.equal = FALSE) used. Otherwise, Student's t-test (var.equal = TRUE) applied alongside one-way ANOVA confirmatory check. returned list consumed internally analyze.gene().

title: Perform Adaptive T-Test — adaptive.t.test

what: Adjust P-Values for Multiple Comparisons — adjust.pvalues
title: Adjust P-Values for Multiple Comparisons — adjust.pvalues
text: Applies multiple testing correction vector raw p-values, reducing false discovery rate many genes tested simultaneously.

title: Adjust P-Values for Multiple Comparisons — adjust.pvalues
Adjust P-Values for Multiple Comparisons — adjust.pvalues
text: p.values Numeric vector raw p-values, one per gene probe. method Character. Correction method passed p.adjust(). Common options "BH" (Benjamini-Hochberg), "bonferroni", "holm". Default "BH".

title: Adjust P-Values for Multiple Comparisons — adjust.pvalues
text: numeric vector adjusted p-values, length order input. BH method values represent estimated false discovery rate (FDR); Bonferroni Holm represent family-wise error rate.

title: Adjust P-Values for Multiple Comparisons — adjust.pvalues
text: analyze.gene() applied across many probes loop, test performed independently p-values corrected multiplicity. function applied resulting p-value vector interpreting significance. BH correction recommended exploratory genomic analyses; Bonferroni conservative suited confirmatory settings. genome-wide analysis, prefer run.limma.de() handles correction internally.

title: Adjust P-Values for Multiple Comparisons — adjust.pvalues
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ geo <- extract.expression(load.geo.soft("GDS3268.soft")) probe.ids <- find.probe.by.gene(geo$gene, c("GENE1", "GENE2", "GENE3"))  raw.pvals <- sapply(probe.ids, function(id) {   expr <- get.gene.expression(geo$expression, id)   df <- build.analysis.df(expr, geo$phenotype, geo$gene)   analyze.gene(df)$p.value }) adj.pvals <- adjust.pvalues(raw.pvals, method = "BH") } # }

what: Analyze Gene Expression Between Groups — analyze.gene
title: Analyze Gene Expression Between Groups — analyze.gene
text: Performs comprehensive statistical analysis comparing gene expression two groups. Integrates parametric nonparametric testing, effect size estimation, bootstrapped confidence intervals, heuristic biological relevance assessment.

title: Analyze Gene Expression Between Groups — analyze.gene
Analyze Gene Expression Between Groups — analyze.gene
text: df data frame produced build.analysis.df(), containing least two columns: expression Numeric vector gene expression values. group Character factor vector exactly two group labels. data frame contains multiple genes (.e., gene column one unique value), expression values genes pooled together. Subset data frame single gene calling function per-gene results. alpha Numeric. Significance threshold. Default 0.05. n.boot Integer. Number bootstrap resamples confidence interval. Default 1000.

title: Analyze Gene Expression Between Groups — analyze.gene
text: named list nine elements: p.value Numeric. P-value adaptive t-test. effect.size Numeric. Cohen's d. effect.size.class Character. Qualitative magnitude label classify.effect.size(). confidence.interval Named list lower upper bounds bootstrapped CI around Cohen's d. nonparametric.p Numeric. P-value Wilcoxon rank-sum test. robustness Character. Agreement assessment parametric nonparametric tests. biological.relevance Character. Heuristic relevance label flag.biological.relevance(). interpretation Character. Human-readable summary combining . raw List. Raw output parametric nonparametric tests inspection.

title: Analyze Gene Expression Between Groups — analyze.gene
text: function integrates multiple statistical perspectives provide complete picture group differences p-value alone. result designed feed directly plot.gene.analysis() visualization.

title: Analyze Gene Expression Between Groups — analyze.gene
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ geo <- extract.expression(load.geo.soft("GDS3268.soft")) probe <- find.probe.by.gene(geo$gene, "mucin 20, cell surface associated") expr <- get.gene.expression(geo$expression, probe) df <- build.analysis.df(expr, geo$phenotype, geo$gene) result <- analyze.gene(df) cat(result$interpretation) } # }

title: Build Analysis Data Frame — build.analysis.df
text: Combines multi-gene expression matrix sample metadata long-format, analysis-ready data frame. row represents one gene-sample observation, human-readable gene names resolved annotation data frame.

title: Build Analysis Data Frame — build.analysis.df
Build Analysis Data Frame — build.analysis.df
text: expr.matrix Numeric matrix gene expression values returned get.gene.expression(). Rows probe IDs, columns sample IDs. phenotype Data frame sample metadata returned extract.expression()$phenotype. Row names must correspond column names expr.matrix. genes Data frame gene annotations returned extract.expression()$gene. Row indices must correspond probe IDs (row names) expr.matrix. Used resolve probe IDs human-readable gene names stored "Gene symbol" column. group.col Character. Name column phenotype use grouping variable. Default "disease.state".

title: Build Analysis Data Frame — build.analysis.df
text: long-format data frame one row per gene-sample pair three columns: gene Character. Human-readable gene name resolved gene annotation data frame. expression Numeric. Expression value gene-sample pair. group Character factor. Group label sample, renamed group.col column phenotype consistency downstream functions. Rows NaN NA expression values removed.

title: Build Analysis Data Frame — build.analysis.df
text: function pivots expr.matrix wide format (probes x samples) long format, merges sample metadata sample ID, resolves probe IDs gene names using annotation data frame, selects three columns needed analysis. output standard input analyze.gene(), gene.analysis.plot(), fit.lasso(). Requires tidyr pivot step. Ensure tidyr listed Imports package DESCRIPTION.

title: Build Analysis Data Frame — build.analysis.df
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ geo <- extract.expression(load.geo.soft("GDS3268.soft")) probe <- find.probe.by.gene(geo$gene, c("MUC20", "ADH1A")) expr <- get.gene.expression(geo$expression, probe) df <- build.analysis.df(expr, geo$phenotype, geo$gene) head(df) } # }

title: Classify Effect Size — classify.effect.size
text: Categorizes absolute Cohen's d value qualitative magnitude label using conventional thresholds.

title: Classify Effect Size — classify.effect.size
Classify Effect Size — classify.effect.size
text: d Numeric. Cohen's d effect size value (signed unsigned).

title: Classify Effect Size — classify.effect.size
text: Character string. One "negligible", "small", "moderate", "large" based absolute value d, using thresholds: negligible < 0.2 <= small < 0.5 <= moderate < 0.8 <= large.

title: Classify Effect Size — classify.effect.size

what: Compute Confidence Interval for Effect Size — compute.ci
title: Compute Confidence Interval for Effect Size — compute.ci
text: Estimates confidence interval Cohen's d using bootstrap resampling. provides measure uncertainty around effect size estimate, especially useful sample sizes small distributions deviate normality.

title: Compute Confidence Interval for Effect Size — compute.ci
Compute Confidence Interval for Effect Size — compute.ci
text: df data frame containing least two columns: expression Numeric vector gene expression values. group Character factor vector indicating group membership. alpha Numeric. Significance level used compute confidence interval bounds. Default 0.05, yielding 95 percent CI. n.boot Integer. Number bootstrap resamples perform. Default 1000.

title: Compute Confidence Interval for Effect Size — compute.ci
text: named list two elements: lower Lower bound bootstrapped confidence interval. upper Upper bound bootstrapped confidence interval.

title: Compute Confidence Interval for Effect Size — compute.ci
text: function repeatedly resamples input data replacement recomputes Cohen's d resample. confidence interval derived empirical distribution bootstrapped effect sizes using percentile method.

title: Compute Confidence Interval for Effect Size — compute.ci
Compute Effect Size (Cohen's d) — compute.effect.size
text: Calculates standardized mean difference two groups.

title: Compute Effect Size (Cohen's d) — compute.effect.size
Compute Effect Size (Cohen's d) — compute.effect.size
text: df data frame containing least two columns: expression Numeric vector gene expression values. group Character factor vector exactly two group labels, produced build.analysis.df().

title: Compute Effect Size (Cohen's d) — compute.effect.size
text: Numeric. Cohen's d value. Positive values indicate first group (alphabetically) higher mean expression; negative values indicate second group higher.

what: Plot Gene Co-expression Correlation Heatmap — correlation.heatmap.plot
title: Plot Gene Co-expression Correlation Heatmap — correlation.heatmap.plot
text: Visualizes gene--gene correlation matrix clustered heatmap, revealing groups co-expressed genes within candidate gene set.

title: Plot Gene Co-expression Correlation Heatmap — correlation.heatmap.plot
Plot Gene Co-expression Correlation Heatmap — correlation.heatmap.plot
text: cor.matrix Numeric matrix returned gene.correlation.matrix(). Must square symmetric row/column names corresponding probe IDs. gene.names Character vector gene names use axis labels place probe IDs. Must length order cor.matrix rows. Default NULL, uses probe IDs.

title: Plot Gene Co-expression Correlation Heatmap — correlation.heatmap.plot
text: pheatmap object displaying correlation matrix hierarchical clustering applied rows columns. Color scale runs blue (strong negative correlation) white (correlation) red (strong positive correlation).

title: Plot Gene Co-expression Correlation Heatmap — correlation.heatmap.plot
text: Hierarchical clustering correlation matrix groups genes similar co-expression patterns visible blocks heatmap. blocks often correspond genes pathway shared regulatory control. function targeted companion gene.correlation.matrix() pre-selected gene set, complements genome-wide network view produced WGCNA.

title: Plot Gene Co-expression Correlation Heatmap — correlation.heatmap.plot
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ probe.ids <- sapply(c("BRCA1", "TP53", "MYC", "EGFR"), function(g) {   find.probe.by.gene(genes, g) }) cor.mat <- gene.correlation.matrix(expression, probe.ids) correlation.heatmap.plot(cor.mat, gene.names = c("BRCA1", "TP53", "MYC", "EGFR")) } # }

what: Extract Expression and Metadata from GEO Dataset — extract.expression
title: Extract Expression and Metadata from GEO Dataset — extract.expression
text: Decomposes ExpressionSet object core components, returning named list used primary data structure throughout BioUtils.

title: Extract Expression and Metadata from GEO Dataset — extract.expression
Extract Expression and Metadata from GEO Dataset — extract.expression
text: eset ExpressionSet object returned load.geo.soft().

title: Extract Expression and Metadata from GEO Dataset — extract.expression
text: named list three elements: expression Numeric matrix gene expression values. Rows probe IDs, columns sample IDs. phenotype Data frame sample metadata (e.g., disease state, age, tissue). Row names sample IDs matching column names expression. gene Data frame probe-level gene annotations (e.g., gene symbol, gene title, chromosomal location). Row names probe IDs matching row names expression.

title: Extract Expression and Metadata from GEO Dataset — extract.expression
text: three returned components form basis downstream BioUtils workflows. gene data frame passed find.probe..gene() probe lookup, expression passed get.gene.expression(), phenotype passed build.analysis.df() group labeling.

title: Extract Expression and Metadata from GEO Dataset — extract.expression
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ eset <- load.geo.soft("GDS3268.soft") geo <- extract.expression(eset) head(geo$phenotype) dim(geo$expression) } # }

what: Find Probe IDs by Gene Name — find.probe.by.gene
title: Find Probe IDs by Gene Name — find.probe.by.gene
text: Searches gene annotation data frame find probe IDs corresponding one gene names descriptions.

title: Find Probe IDs by Gene Name — find.probe.by.gene
Find Probe IDs by Gene Name — find.probe.by.gene
text: genes Data frame gene annotations returned extract.expression()$gene. second column (index 2) expected contain gene titles descriptions. gene.names Character vector one gene names descriptions search . Matching exact case-sensitive.

title: Find Probe IDs by Gene Name — find.probe.by.gene
text: Integer vector probe IDs corresponding matched genes. length vector equals number matches found. Returns empty integer vector matches found.

title: Find Probe IDs by Gene Name — find.probe.by.gene
text: Probe IDs integer row names genes annotation data frame. IDs used directly index rows expression matrix get.gene.expression(). multiple gene names supplied, matching probe IDs returned vector, passed -get.gene.expression() retrieve multi-row expression matrix.

title: Find Probe IDs by Gene Name — find.probe.by.gene
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ geo <- extract.expression(load.geo.soft("GDS3268.soft"))  # Single gene probe <- find.probe.by.gene(geo$gene, "mucin 20, cell surface associated")  # Multiple genes probes <- find.probe.by.gene(geo$gene, c(   "mucin 20, cell surface associated",   "alcohol dehydrogenase 1A (class I), alpha polypeptide" )) } # }

what: Fit LASSO Regression for Multi-Gene Biomarker Discovery — fit.lasso
title: Fit LASSO Regression for Multi-Gene Biomarker Discovery — fit.lasso
text: Applies cross-validated LASSO logistic regression identify sparse set genes jointly predict binary phenotype. Unlike univariate tests, LASSO accounts joint contribution genes simultaneously, automatically penalizing redundant predictors.

title: Fit LASSO Regression for Multi-Gene Biomarker Discovery — fit.lasso
Fit LASSO Regression for Multi-Gene Biomarker Discovery — fit.lasso
text: expression.matrix Numeric matrix gene expression values returned extract.expression()$expression. Rows probes, columns samples. matrix transposed internally samples become rows required glmnet. phenotype.vector Factor integer vector binary phenotype labels, one per sample (e.g., 0 control, 1 disease). Must length number columns expression.matrix. alpha Numeric. Elastic net mixing parameter. 1 (default) gives pure LASSO; 0 gives Ridge; values 0 1 give elastic net. nfolds Integer. Number cross-validation folds. Default 10.

title: Fit LASSO Regression for Multi-Gene Biomarker Discovery — fit.lasso
text: cv.glmnet object. Key elements: lambda.min Lambda minimum cross-validated error. lambda.1se Largest lambda within 1 SE minimum (sparser model). glmnet.fit full sequence fitted models across lambda values. Extract selected genes coef(fit, s = "lambda.1se"); probes non-zero coefficients LASSO-selected biomarkers.

title: Fit LASSO Regression for Multi-Gene Biomarker Discovery — fit.lasso
text: LASSO suited high-dimensional genomic data number genes far exceeds samples. shrinks coefficients exactly zero, retaining genes independent predictive value. complements run.limma.de() - limma ranks genes individual differential expression, LASSO identifies minimal subset non-redundant joint predictive signal.

title: Fit LASSO Regression for Multi-Gene Biomarker Discovery — fit.lasso
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ geo <- extract.expression(load.geo.soft("GDS3268.soft")) phenotype.binary <- ifelse(geo$phenotype$disease.state == "disease", 1, 0) lasso.fit <- fit.lasso(geo$expression, phenotype.binary) selected <- coef(lasso.fit, s = "lambda.1se") selected[selected[, 1] != 0, ] } # }

what: Assess Biological Relevance of Gene Expression Differences — flag.biological.relevance
title: Assess Biological Relevance of Gene Expression Differences — flag.biological.relevance
text: Provides heuristic interpretation whether statistically significant difference gene expression likely biologically meaningful, based effect size magnitude p-value.

title: Assess Biological Relevance of Gene Expression Differences — flag.biological.relevance
Assess Biological Relevance of Gene Expression Differences — flag.biological.relevance
text: effect.size Numeric. Cohen's d standardized effect size. p.value Numeric. P-value statistical test. alpha Numeric. Significance threshold used p-value comparison. Default 0.05.

title: Assess Biological Relevance of Gene Expression Differences — flag.biological.relevance
text: Character string. One three heuristic labels: "Potentially biologically meaningful" p < alpha |d| > 0.5 "Statistically significant small effect" p < alpha |d| <= 0.5 "strong evidence biological relevance" p >= alpha

title: Assess Biological Relevance of Gene Expression Differences — flag.biological.relevance
text: rules heuristic intended guidance rather definitive biological conclusions. thresholds effect size (0.5) significance conventional starting points; domain knowledge inform interpretation. function called internally analyze.gene() using alpha passed function.

title: Assess Biological Relevance of Gene Expression Differences — flag.biological.relevance

what: Plot Gene Expression with Automatic Statistical Analysis — gene.analysis.plot
title: Plot Gene Expression with Automatic Statistical Analysis — gene.analysis.plot
text: Generates annotated visualization gene expression two groups. single-gene data frame, plot annotated statistical results analyze.gene(). multi-gene data frame, plot faceted gene annotations omitted, use analyze.gene() gene subset per-gene statistics.

title: Plot Gene Expression with Automatic Statistical Analysis — gene.analysis.plot
Plot Gene Expression with Automatic Statistical Analysis — gene.analysis.plot
text: df data frame produced build.analysis.df(), containing: expression Numeric vector gene expression values. group Character factor vector exactly two group labels. gene Character vector gene names. Optional; present containing one unique value, enables faceted multi-gene plotting. alpha Numeric. Significance level passed analyze.gene(). Default 0.05. used single-gene mode. n.boot Integer. Number bootstrap resamples confidence interval, passed analyze.gene(). Default 1000. used single-gene mode. show.points Logical. Whether overlay jittered individual sample points violin/boxplot. Default TRUE.

title: Plot Gene Expression with Automatic Statistical Analysis — gene.analysis.plot
text: ggplot object. single-gene mode plot includes text annotation p-value, Cohen's d, bootstrapped CI, subtitle full interpretation string. multi-gene mode plot faceted gene statistical annotation.

title: Plot Gene Expression with Automatic Statistical Analysis — gene.analysis.plot
text: Single-gene mode triggered gene column absent contains exactly one unique value. Multi-gene mode triggered gene contains one unique value, producing facet_wrap(~ gene) layout. plot theme consistent across modes matches style conventions BioUtils package.

title: Plot Gene Expression with Automatic Statistical Analysis — gene.analysis.plot
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ geo <- extract.expression(load.geo.soft("GDS3268.soft"))  # Single-gene plot with statistical annotation probe <- find.probe.by.gene(geo$gene, "mucin 20, cell surface associated") expr <- get.gene.expression(geo$expression, probe) df <- build.analysis.df(expr, geo$phenotype, geo$gene) gene.analysis.plot(df)  # Multi-gene faceted plot probes <- find.probe.by.gene(geo$gene, c(   "mucin 20, cell surface associated",   "alcohol dehydrogenase 1A (class I), alpha polypeptide" )) expr.multi <- get.gene.expression(geo$expression, probes) df.multi <- build.analysis.df(expr.multi, geo$phenotype, geo$gene) gene.analysis.plot(df.multi) } # }

what: Compute Pairwise Gene Co-expression Correlation Matrix — gene.correlation.matrix
title: Compute Pairwise Gene Co-expression Correlation Matrix — gene.correlation.matrix
text: Calculates pairwise correlations set genes across samples, producing symmetric correlation matrix quantifies co-expression relationships.

title: Compute Pairwise Gene Co-expression Correlation Matrix — gene.correlation.matrix
Compute Pairwise Gene Co-expression Correlation Matrix — gene.correlation.matrix
text: expression.matrix Numeric matrix gene expression values returned extract.expression()$expression. Rows probes, columns samples. probe.ids Integer vector probe IDs include, returned find.probe..gene(). method Character. Correlation method: "pearson" (default), "spearman", "kendall". Spearman recommended distributions skewed outliers concern.

title: Compute Pairwise Gene Co-expression Correlation Matrix — gene.correlation.matrix
text: symmetric numeric matrix dimensions length(probe.ids) x length(probe.ids), cell contains pairwise correlation coefficient across samples. Diagonal values 1. Row column names correspond probe IDs.

title: Compute Pairwise Gene Co-expression Correlation Matrix — gene.correlation.matrix
text: Co-expression correlations capture whether two genes tend simultaneously - -regulated across samples, can suggest shared regulatory control pathway membership. resulting matrix direct input plot.correlation.heatmap().

title: Compute Pairwise Gene Co-expression Correlation Matrix — gene.correlation.matrix
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ geo <- extract.expression(load.geo.soft("GDS3268.soft")) probe.ids <- find.probe.by.gene(geo$gene, c("BRCA1", "TP53", "MYC")) cor.mat <- gene.correlation.matrix(geo$expression, probe.ids, method = "spearman") plot.correlation.heatmap(cor.mat) } # }

what: Extract Gene Expression by Probe ID — get.gene.expression
title: Extract Gene Expression by Probe ID — get.gene.expression
text: Retrieves expression values one genes expression matrix using integer probe IDs.

title: Extract Gene Expression by Probe ID — get.gene.expression
Extract Gene Expression by Probe ID — get.gene.expression
text: expression Numeric matrix gene expression values returned extract.expression()$expression. Rows probe IDs, columns sample IDs. probe.id Integer vector one probe IDs returned find.probe..gene().

title: Extract Gene Expression by Probe ID — get.gene.expression
text: numeric matrix one row per probe one column per sample. Row names probe IDs; column names sample IDs. single probe ID supplied result still matrix (vector), ensuring build.analysis.df() receives consistent input type.

title: Extract Gene Expression by Probe ID — get.gene.expression
text: returned matrix direct input build.analysis.df(). Using .matrix() ensures single-probe case returns one-row matrix rather named vector, keeps pivot_longer step build.analysis.df() consistent regardless many genes queried.

title: Extract Gene Expression by Probe ID — get.gene.expression
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ geo <- extract.expression(load.geo.soft("GDS3268.soft")) probes <- find.probe.by.gene(geo$gene, c(   "mucin 20, cell surface associated",   "alcohol dehydrogenase 1A (class I), alpha polypeptide" )) expr <- get.gene.expression(geo$expression, probes) dim(expr)  # probes x samples } # }

what: Resolve Probe IDs to Gene Names — get.gene.name
title: Resolve Probe IDs to Gene Names — get.gene.name
text: Converts one integer probe IDs back human-readable gene title strings short gene symbols using gene annotation data frame. inverse operation find.probe..gene().

title: Resolve Probe IDs to Gene Names — get.gene.name
Resolve Probe IDs to Gene Names — get.gene.name
text: genes Data frame gene annotations returned extract.expression()$gene. Must contain columns "ID", "Gene title", "Gene symbol". probe.id Integer character vector one probe IDs resolve, returned find.probe..gene(). use.symbols Logical. FALSE (default), returns full descriptive gene titles "Gene title" column, suitable interpretation reporting. TRUE, returns short gene symbols "Gene symbol" column, suitable plot axis labels downstream tool expects standard gene symbols run.gsea().

title: Resolve Probe IDs to Gene Names — get.gene.name
text: Character vector gene names corresponding supplied probe IDs. Returns empty strings "" probes annotation, filtered (result != "").

title: Resolve Probe IDs to Gene Names — get.gene.name
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ geo <- extract.expression(load.geo.soft(accession = "GDS507",                                         log.transform = TRUE)) de.results <- run.limma.de(geo) top.probes <- rownames(head(de.results, 10))  # Full titles for reporting top.titles  <- get.gene.name(geo$gene, top.probes)  # Short symbols for plot labels and GSEA top.symbols <- get.gene.name(geo$gene, top.probes, use.symbols = TRUE) } # }

what: Load GEO Dataset from SOFT File — load.geo.soft
title: Load GEO Dataset from SOFT File — load.geo.soft
text: Imports GEO dataset converts structured ExpressionSet object. local SOFT file found file.path loaded directly. Otherwise dataset downloaded NCBI GEO using accession cached tempdir().

title: Load GEO Dataset from SOFT File — load.geo.soft
code: load.geo.soft(file.path = NULL, accession = NULL, log.transform = FALSE)

title: Load GEO Dataset from SOFT File — load.geo.soft
text: file.path Character. Path local GEO SOFT file. file exist accession provided, dataset downloaded automatically. accession Character. GEO accession ID (e.g., "GDS507"). used file.path exist. Default NULL. log.transform Logical. Whether apply log2 transformation expression matrix import. Default FALSE.

title: Load GEO Dataset from SOFT File — load.geo.soft
text: ExpressionSet object use extract.expression().

title: Load GEO Dataset from SOFT File — load.geo.soft
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Load from a local file eset <- load.geo.soft("GDS507.soft")  # Download automatically if not found locally eset <- load.geo.soft("GDS507.soft", accession = "GDS507", log.transform = TRUE)  # Download without a local file at all eset <- load.geo.soft(NULL, accession = "GDS507", log.transform = TRUE) } # }

title: Perform Nonparametric Group Comparison — nonparametric.test
text: Conducts Wilcoxon rank-sum test (Mann-Whitney U test) compare expression values two groups without assuming normality.

title: Perform Nonparametric Group Comparison — nonparametric.test
Perform Nonparametric Group Comparison — nonparametric.test
text: df data frame containing least two columns: expression Numeric vector gene expression values. group Character factor vector exactly two group labels.

title: Perform Nonparametric Group Comparison — nonparametric.test
text: named list two elements: p.value Numeric. P-value Wilcoxon rank-sum test. test Character. Name test used ("Wilcoxon rank-sum").

title: Perform Nonparametric Group Comparison — nonparametric.test
text: test useful robustness check parametric methods t-test, especially data skewed contain outliers. result used analyze.gene() populate robustness field return value.

title: Perform Nonparametric Group Comparison — nonparametric.test

what: Plot PCA of Sample Expression Profiles — pca.plot
title: Plot PCA of Sample Expression Profiles — pca.plot
text: Reduces dimensionality full expression matrix using Principal Component Analysis plots samples first two principal component axes, colored phenotype variable. Useful quality control, batch effect detection, exploratory assessment sample clustering.

title: Plot PCA of Sample Expression Profiles — pca.plot
code: pca.plot(   expression.matrix,   phenotype,   color.by = "disease.state",   scale = TRUE )

title: Plot PCA of Sample Expression Profiles — pca.plot
text: expression.matrix Matrix gene expression values. Rows probes, columns samples. phenotype Data frame sample metadata, returned extract.expression()$phenotype. color.Character. Column name phenotype use point coloring. Default "disease.state". scale Logical. Whether scale probes unit variance PCA. Default TRUE.

title: Plot PCA of Sample Expression Profiles — pca.plot
text: ggplot object showing samples points PC1 vs PC2 space, colored specified phenotype variable. percentage variance explained shown axis label.

title: Plot PCA of Sample Expression Profiles — pca.plot
text: PCA typically applied whole-dataset level gene filtering, making useful first diagnostic load.geo.soft() extract.expression(). Tight clustering samples disease state PC1/PC2 space suggests strong detectable expression signal. Unexpected clustering batch, sex, covariates can indicate need covariate adjustment downstream run.limma.de() models.

title: Plot PCA of Sample Expression Profiles — pca.plot

what: Run Gene Set Enrichment Analysis — run.gsea
title: Run Gene Set Enrichment Analysis — run.gsea
text: Tests whether predefined gene sets (e.g., pathways GO terms) systematically enriched among genes ranked differential expression, using fgseaMultilevel algorithm fgsea.

title: Run Gene Set Enrichment Analysis — run.gsea
Run Gene Set Enrichment Analysis — run.gsea
text: de.results Data frame returned run.limma.de(). Must contain logFC column. Row names probe IDs. genes Data frame gene annotations returned extract.expression()$gene. Must contain columns "ID" probe identifiers "Gene symbol" gene symbols matching format used pathway databases MSigDB. pathways Named list character vectors, element gene set names pathway labels. MSigDB GO gene sets format can loaded via msigdbr. min.size Integer. Minimum number genes required gene set tested. Default 15. max.size Integer. Maximum gene set size. Default 500.

title: Run Gene Set Enrichment Analysis — run.gsea
text: data frame one row per tested pathway, containing: pathway Gene set name. pval Nominal p-value. padj BH-adjusted p-value across tested pathways. NES Normalized enrichment score. Positive values indicate enrichment among upregulated genes; negative values indicate enrichment among downregulated genes. size Number genes pathway present ranked list. leadingEdge List-column genes driving enrichment score.

title: Run Gene Set Enrichment Analysis — run.gsea
text: Uses fgseaMultilevel algorithm, estimates p-values using adaptive multilevel splitting Monte Carlo approach. accurate original permutation-based fgseaSimple require fixed permutation count. GSEA operates full ranked gene list run.limma.de() rather filtered subset, preserving information genes. Probe IDs TopTable row names resolved gene symbols via genes annotation data frame matching pathway gene sets. Probes gene annotation silently dropped ranked list, matched pathway. leadingEdge genes strong candidates follow-analyze.gene() gene.analysis.plot().

title: Run Gene Set Enrichment Analysis — run.gsea
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ library(msigdbr) geo <- extract.expression(load.geo.soft(accession = "GDS507",                                                  log.transform = TRUE)) hallmark.df <- msigdbr(species = "Homo sapiens", category = "H") pathways <- split(hallmark.df$gene_symbol, hallmark.df$gs_name) de.results <- run.limma.de(geo) gsea.results <- run.gsea(de.results, geo$gene, pathways) head(gsea.results[order(gsea.results$padj), ]) } # }

what: Run Differential Expression Analysis Using limma — run.limma.de
title: Run Differential Expression Analysis Using limma — run.limma.de
text: Performs genome-wide differential expression analysis across probes using limma linear modeling framework. recommended approach microarray data GEO, borrows variance information across genes produce stable estimates gene--gene t-tests.

title: Run Differential Expression Analysis Using limma — run.limma.de
Run Differential Expression Analysis Using limma — run.limma.de
text: geo Named list returned extract.expression(), containing minimum $expression (probe x sample matrix) $phenotype (sample metadata data frame). condition.col Character. Name column geo$phenotype use grouping variable. Default "disease.state". adjust.method Character. P-value correction method passed limma::topTable(). Default "BH" (Benjamini-Hochberg FDR).

title: Run Differential Expression Analysis Using limma — run.limma.de
text: data frame (TopTable) one row per probe, sorted evidence differential expression, containing: logFC Log2 fold-change conditions. AveExpr Average log2 expression across samples. t Moderated t-statistic. P.Value Raw p-value. adj.P.Val FDR-adjusted p-value (BH default). B Log-odds gene differentially expressed.

title: Run Differential Expression Analysis Using limma — run.limma.de
text: Unlike running analyze.gene() probe individually, limma fits linear model probes simultaneously applies empirical Bayes shrinkage variance estimates. stabilizes results genes observations standard method microarray DE analysis. returned TopTable primary input downstream functions volcano.plot() run.gsea(), can filtered adj.P.Val logFC thresholds define gene signature.

title: Run Differential Expression Analysis Using limma — run.limma.de
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ geo <- extract.expression(load.geo.soft("GDS3268.soft")) de.results <- run.limma.de(geo, condition.col = "disease.state") head(de.results) } # }

what: Plot Volcano Plot from Differential Expression Results — volcano.plot
title: Plot Volcano Plot from Differential Expression Results — volcano.plot
text: Generates volcano plot visualizing relationship statistical significance fold-change magnitude probes differential expression analysis. Points colored whether exceed user-defined significance fold-change thresholds.

title: Plot Volcano Plot from Differential Expression Results — volcano.plot
Plot Volcano Plot from Differential Expression Results — volcano.plot
text: de.results Data frame returned run.limma.de(), containing minimum columns logFC adj.P.Val. fc.threshold Numeric. Absolute log2 fold-change cutoff labeling genes differentially expressed. Default 1 (.e., 2-fold). fdr.threshold Numeric. Adjusted p-value cutoff. Default 0.05.

title: Plot Volcano Plot from Differential Expression Results — volcano.plot
text: ggplot object showing probe point logFC x-axis -log10(adj.P.Val) y-axis. Points colored upregulated, downregulated, significant. Dashed threshold lines drawn specified cutoffs.

title: Plot Volcano Plot from Differential Expression Results — volcano.plot
text: volcano plot standard summary visualization TopTable run.limma.de() serves genome-wide complement gene.analysis.plot(), visualizes single gene. Probes appear upper corners (high fold-change, low FDR) strongest candidates follow-analyze.gene() inclusion gene signature run.gsea().

title: Plot Volcano Plot from Differential Expression Results — volcano.plot
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ de.results <- run.limma.de(eset, condition.col = "disease.state") volcano.plot(de.results, fc.threshold = 1, fdr.threshold = 0.05) } # }
